name: Rumney Main Cliff
notes: Sport climbing mecca. West-facing schist dries quickly after rain.

name: Rumney Waimea
notes: South-facing, sun-soaked. Cooks in summer, magic in shoulder seasons.

name: Cathedral Ledge
notes: Classic granite trad and sport. Seeps after heavy rain, dries slow.

name: Cannon Cliff
notes: Big alpine face. Cold, exposed, weather-sensitive. East-facing — morning sun.

name: Pawtuckaway
notes: Bouldering area. Forested, shaded boulders dry slower in humid weather.

name: Red River Gorge
notes: America's premier sport climbing destination. Overhanging pocketed sandstone. Dries quickly — rain drains fast off steep walls.

name: New River Gorge
notes: Steep sandstone walls in America's newest National Park. North-facing — cooler temps. 1,400+ routes.

name: Seneca Rocks
notes: Stunning quartzite fins rising 900 ft from the valley. Trad-focused. Exposed to wind, dries fast.

name: Shawangunk Mountains
notes: The Gunks — legendary horizontal cracks and overhangs. South-facing, seeps after rain, needs 24–48h to dry.

name: Devil's Lake
notes: Midwest's best climbing. Quartzite bluffs above a stunning lake. Dries relatively fast.

name: Horseshoe Canyon Ranch
notes: Best sport climbing in the Midwest. Limestone-like sandstone. South-facing walls dry extremely fast after rain.

name: Foster Falls
notes: Gorgeous sandstone walls next to a waterfall. Tennessee's top sport climbing destination.

name: Looking Glass Rock
notes: Classic Carolina granite dome. Slab and face climbing. Wet streaks linger — needs dry spell.

name: Stone Mountain
notes: Giant granite dome in NC. Smooth slabs and arches. South-facing dries fast.

name: Yosemite Valley
notes: World-famous big wall and crack climbing. Spring seepage on big walls. Best: April-June, Sept-Oct.

name: Tuolumne Meadows
notes: High Sierra slabs and domes. Short season (July–Sept). Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer.

name: Joshua Tree
notes: Desert trad and face climbing paradise. 8,000+ routes. Best Oct–May. Hot in summer.

name: Tahquitz Rock
notes: Historic Southern California granite. Birthplace of the Yosemite Decimal System. Cool temps year-round due to elevation.

name: Smith Rock
notes: Birthplace of American sport climbing. Rhyolite tuff dries fast. Wind can be strong. Best Apr–Oct.

name: Index Town Walls
notes: Steep, burly crack climbing in the Pacific Northwest. Wet climate — needs extended dry weather to be climbable.

name: Leavenworth Boulders
notes: Granite crags in a Bavarian-themed town. East of the Cascades — drier than the coast. Spring and fall best.

name: City of Rocks
notes: Ancient granite spires in southern Idaho. Dry climate dries fast. High desert altitude — cool evenings year-round.

name: Eldorado Canyon
notes: Colorado's premier trad area. South Boulder Creek canyon. Seeps after rain, moderate drying time.

name: Rifle Mountain Park
notes: World-class steep limestone sport climbing. Overhangs shed water well — often climbable same day as rain.

name: Lumpy Ridge
notes: Rocky Mountain trad classic. Granite domes above Estes Park. Afternoon thunderstorms July–Aug.

name: Ten Sleep Canyon
notes: World-class limestone sport climbing in Wyoming. Caves and overhangs stay dry. Sunny and arid — dries fast.

name: Vedauwoo
notes: Erratic granite boulders with wild off-width and wide cracks. Windy and arid. Dries very quickly.

name: Maple Canyon
notes: Unique cobblestone conglomerate. Pockets everywhere. Shaded canyon, cool in summer. Seeps minimally.

name: American Fork Canyon
notes: World-class limestone 30 min from Salt Lake City. Steep pockets and edges. Seeps after rain — needs 24h.

name: Joe's Valley
notes: World-class bouldering destination. High-quality sandstone. Dries fast in Utah's arid climate. Best fall and spring.

name: Red Rock Canyon
notes: Stunning red sandstone 20 min from Las Vegas. Desert-dry conditions, excellent friction. Best Oct–May.

name: Hueco Tanks
notes: World-class bouldering on pocketed syenite. Permit system required. Best Nov–Mar. Hot in summer.

name: Queen Creek Canyon
notes: Phoenix-area sport and trad climbing. Quartzite walls dry very fast in desert climate. Hot summers, ideal winters.

name: Cochise Stronghold
notes: Historic Apache granite in SE Arizona. Secluded, world-class trad climbing. Monsoon July–Sept — avoid or check conditions.

name: Jack's Canyon
notes: Arizona's hidden limestone gem. Long, sustained sport routes. Desert-arid, dries instantly.

name: Enchanted Rock
notes: Largest pink granite batholith in the US. Slab and dome climbing. Permit required. Best Oct–Apr.

name: Crowders Mountain
notes: Piedmont quartzite pinnacles near Charlotte. Good variety of trad routes. Dries quickly after rain.

name: Horse Pens 40
notes: Southern Appalachian sandstone bouldering on a private preserve. Sit starts on big moves. Humid in summer.

name: Carderock
notes: DC-area classic schist walls above the Potomac. Short but quality routes. Seeps after rain, dries in a day.

name: Breakneck Ridge
notes: Hudson Valley trad above Metro-North train stop. Schist can be lichenous. Best spring and fall.

name: Sam's Throne
notes: Ozark sandstone bluffs with everything from 5.6 to 5.14. Camping on-site. Dries fast in dry weather.

name: Tallulah Gorge
notes: Georgia quartzite in a dramatic river gorge. Permit required. Humidity can be high — check forecast.
